g.data-narration: Welcome to Basic Connectives and Truth Tables. This fundamental topic forms the core of propositional logic in discrete mathematics.
Propositional logic provides a formal framework for representing statements that are either true or false, essential for computing.
g.text: A formal framework for representing and manipulating logical statements
that are either TRUE or FALSE (but not both).

g.data-narration: A proposition is a declarative statement with a definite truth value - either true or false, but never both.
g.text: A declarative statement that is either DEFINITELY TRUE (T) or

g.data-narration: Negation (¬) reverses truth value: if p is true, ¬p is false, and vice versa. The simplest logical connective.
g.text: Reverses the truth value of a proposition.

g.data-narration: Conjunction (∧) is true only when BOTH p and q are true. Represents logical AND operation.
g.text: True only when BOTH p and q are true.

g.data-narration: Disjunction (∨) is true when AT LEAST ONE operand is true. This is inclusive OR - true even if both are true.
g.text: True when AT LEAST ONE is true (inclusive OR).

g.data-narration: Implication (p → q) is false ONLY when p is TRUE and q is FALSE. This is the most tricky connective for students.
g.text: "If p then q" - FALSE only when p=T and q=F.

g.data-narration: Biconditional (p ↔ q) is true when both have the SAME truth value. Represents if-and-only-if relationship.
g.text: "p if and only if q" - TRUE when p and q have SAME value.

g.data-narration: Operator precedence from highest to lowest: Negation > Conjunction > Disjunction > Implication > Biconditional.
g.text: When evaluating without parentheses, follow this order:
Example: p → q ∧ r = p → (q ∧ r)

g.data-narration: Tautology is always true; contradiction is always false; contingency depends on component truth values.
Two propositions are logically equivalent if their truth tables match. Key equivalences include De Morgan's laws.
g.text: Two propositions have identical truth values in ALL cases.

g.data-narration: For 3 propositions (p, q, r), we need 2³=8 rows. Let's build the truth table for (p ∨ q) → ¬r step by step.
g.text: 3 propositions → 2³ = 8 rows. Evaluate step by step:

g.data-narration: Complete truth table summary showing all five connectives with their results for all possible input combinations.
Key exam tips: memorize all truth tables, understand implication's false case, practice De Morgan's laws, count rows correctly.
g.text: Focus on implication (only F when T→F)
Master truth tables to excel in university exams! ✓
